content.problemStatement:
We define the goodness of any array BBB of length MMM as: ∑i=1M−1∣Bi+1−Bi∣\sum_{i = 1}^{M - 1} |B_{i + 1} - B_{i}|∑i=1M−1​∣Bi+1​−Bi​∣ (Here ∣X∣|X|∣X∣ denotes the absolute value of XXX). In particular, goodness of any array of length 111 is 000.

Alice and Bob have an array AAA containing NNN **distinct** elements and they play a game with it.

Alice starts the game after which both the players make moves alternatively. In one move:


A player can **delete** any subarray from AAA such that the goodness of AAA does **not** change.
Formally, the player chooses indices LLL and RRR such that 1≤L≤R≤length(A)1 \le L \le R \le \mathrm{length}(A)1≤L≤R≤length(A) and removes the subarray [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L + 1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​] from AAA such that the goodness of AAA does not change.

For e.g. A=[3,1,2,4]A = [3, 1, 2, 4]A=[3,1,2,4], a player can select L=3,R=3L = 3, R = 3L=3,R=3, then the resulting array will be [3,1,4][3, 1, 4][3,1,4]. Initially, the goodness was ∣1−3∣+∣2−1∣+∣4−2∣=5|1-3| + |2-1| + |4-2| = 5∣1−3∣+∣2−1∣+∣4−2∣=5. After the move, the goodness is ∣1−3∣+∣4−1∣=5|1-3| + |4-1| = 5∣1−3∣+∣4−1∣=5. Here we can observe that the **goodness** does not change.

The player who is not able to make a move loses. If both the players play optimally, determine who out of Alice and Bob will win the game.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the winner of the game if both players play optimally.


You may print each character of `Alice` and `Bob` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `aLiCe`, `ALIce`, `alice` will be considered identical).
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: Alice can not make any moves such that the goodness of the array does not change. Therefore Bob wins.
Test case 2: Alice can choose L=2,R=3L = 2, R = 3L=2,R=3 and remove the subarray [A2,A3][A_2, A_3][A2​,A3​]. The resulting array is [1,4][1, 4][1,4].

Goodness of the array before the operation =∣2−1∣+∣3−2∣+∣4−3∣=3 = |2-1|+|3-2|+|4-3| = 3=∣2−1∣+∣3−2∣+∣4−3∣=3.
Goodness of the array after the operation =∣4−1∣=3 = |4-1| = 3=∣4−1∣=3.

Thus, the array has the same goodness as before. Now Bob can not make any move. Therefore Alice wins.
